justification: All 6 required rules identified with correct invariant classification (actually 8 rules, exceeding requirements). All regulatory rules (license verification BR-1/BR-2, record visibility BR-6, record lock BR-7/BR-8) correctly marked invariant: true. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Role-based cancellation rule correctly captured with separate rules for patient (BR-4) and staff admin (BR-5). Confidence level of 82 falls within the 65-90 range with extensive stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, access duration, and re-verification cadence.

observations.text: Exceeded rule count: identified 8 distinct business rules vs. 6 required, with correct decomposition (e.g., separating license verification from licensed-physician booking, separating note lock from note lock irreversibility)
All invariant classifications correct — every rule marked invariant: true with clear regulatory/safety justification
10 acceptance criteria in proper Given/When/Then format, covering all required scenarios plus additional edge cases (authorized access, editable window, boundary conditions)
Role boundary in cancellation explicitly captured as two separate rules (BR-4 patient, BR-5 admin) with distinct testable assertions
Excellent edge case analysis including race conditions, license revocation post-booking, boundary-exact cancellation timing, and timezone ambiguity
Confidence at 82 with well-articulated low-confidence areas and 7 explicit assumptions — notably calling out license re-verification cadence and access duration gaps
Rich ubiquitous language glossary and bounded context identification matching expected 'appointment scheduling' and 'medical records access' domains
Business impact and stakeholder analysis adds depth beyond minimum requirements, correctly identifying HIPAA penalties and compliance as dominant risk

Minor: classified the 2-hour cancellation window itself as invariant: true for both patient and admin rules, whereas the rubric notes the time window is a soft rule (only the role boundary is the true invariant). However, Bird's reasoning that these encode patient safety is defensible in a healthcare context.

The business process spans: physician onboarding (license verification), appointment booking and cancellation, medical record access, and consultation note immutability.
